coredata.dc:title: Novel visible-light responsive NCQDs-TiO2/PAA/PES photocatalytic membrane with enhanced antifouling properties and self-cleaning performance
coredata.dc:creator: Heng, Zeng Wei
Chong, Woon Chan
Pang, Yean Ling
Sim, Lan Ching
Koo, Chai Hoon
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a novel multifunctional composite membrane with photocatalytic, antifouling and self-cleaning properties were prepared through the surface immobilization of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots modified TiO2 (NCQDs-TiO2) photocatalysts using polyacrylic acid (PAA) as a bridging agent. The surface polymerization of PAA has introduced functional groups for the self-assembly of NCQDs-TiO2 functional layer (denoted as NTO) to improve the hydrophilicity of conventional PES membrane. The modified membranes with varying photocatalyst concentration (0.1, 0.3 and 0.5 wt% NTO) were characterized for their surface chemistry and morphology using SEM-EDX, FTIR, XPS and contact angle analysis. Furthermore, the effect of NCQDs-TiO2 nanocomposite on the membrane performance was assessed in terms of rejection ability, fouling mitigation, self-cleaning and photocatalytic capability using methylene blue as a model pollutant. The characterization results evidenced that the NTO nancomposites were firmly attached on the PES-based supporting layer through the chemical bonding between surface-grafted carboxylic group and Ti atom. The surface hydrophilicity of composite membrane was enormously enhanced following a direct proportionality to NTO loading. The novel NTO/PAA/PES membrane also demonstrated high resistance to MB fouling and was able to revive 93.7% of original water fluxes upon 15 min of visible-light irradiation. Moreover, the modified membranes could efficiently retain 90.9% of MB dye pollutant. The outstanding antifouling and self-cleaning performance were reasonably attributed to the intrinsic hydrophilicity and robust photocatalytic activity of NTO layer. This was further confirmed by the photodegradation of MB wherein the NTO-0.5/PAA/PES membrane degraded 94.9% of MB under 1 hr of visible-light irradiation. With its twofold advantages of membrane filtration and photocatalysis, the visible-light responsive photocatalytic membrane demonstrates huge potential in waste water treatment which could minimize membrane fouling and increase reusability of photocatalyst.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a novel multifunctional composite membrane with photocatalytic, antifouling and self-cleaning properties were prepared through the surface immobilization of nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots modified TiO2 (NCQDs-TiO2) photocatalysts using polyacrylic acid (PAA) as a bridging agent. The surface polymerization of PAA has introduced functional groups for the self-assembly of NCQDs-TiO2 functional layer (denoted as NTO) to improve the hydrophilicity of conventional PES membrane. The modified membranes with varying photocatalyst concentration (0.1, 0.3 and 0.5 wt% NTO) were characterized for their surface chemistry and morphology using SEM-EDX, FTIR, XPS and contact angle analysis. Furthermore, the effect of NCQDs-TiO2 nanocomposite on the membrane performance was assessed in terms of rejection ability, fouling mitigation, self-cleaning and photocatalytic capability using methylene blue as a model pollutant. The characterization results evidenced that the NTO nancomposites were firmly attached on the PES-based supporting layer through the chemical bonding between surface-grafted carboxylic group and Ti atom. The surface hydrophilicity of composite membrane was enormously enhanced following a direct proportionality to NTO loading. The novel NTO/PAA/PES membrane also demonstrated high resistance to MB fouling and was able to revive 93.7% of original water fluxes upon 15 min of visible-light irradiation. Moreover, the modified membranes could efficiently retain 90.9% of MB dye pollutant. The outstanding antifouling and self-cleaning performance were reasonably attributed to the intrinsic hydrophilicity and robust photocatalytic activity of NTO layer. This was further confirmed by the photodegradation of MB wherein the NTO-0.5/PAA/PES membrane degraded 94.9% of MB under 1 hr of visible-light irradiation. With its twofold advantages of membrane filtration and photocatalysis, the visible-light responsive photocatalytic membrane demonstrates huge potential in waste water treatment which could minimize membrane fouling and increase reusability of photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: In the past decade, the demand for membrane technology is steadily growing for separation of various pollutants in water treatment industry. This is likely due to its attractive benefits associated, such as small and compact installation size, low chemical consumption and environmental impact, ease of operation and maintenance, excellent selectivity and separation efficiency as well as high effluent quality [1,2]. Among the diverse materials developed, most commercial membranes are dominated by polymer membrane (polyethersulfone (PES), polyacrylonitrile (PAN), polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF), polysulfone (PSf), polyimide (PI)) owing to their exquisite properties such as superior chemical resistance, high mechanical strength, low production cost, high operational flexibility and easy integration with other processes [3,4]. Despite their great potential in various separation-related industrial processes, the polymeric nature of membrane shows unsatisfying result in membrane fouling. Due to the inherent hydrophobicity and low surface energy of polymer material, these membranes often experience drastic fouling problem resulting from the adsorption of influent pollutants on or within the membrane matrix [5]. The deposition and accumulation of foulants eventually lead to irreversible fouling, and consequently results in sharp decline of flux, frequent cleaning and short membrane life. Furthermore, post-treatment processes are required to disintegrate the concentrates before being discharged to the environment [2]. The extra energy and chemicals required for membrane regeneration, and additional treatment processes have increased the overall operation costs and hence limit its practical application to some extent.

To circumvent the inherent shortcomings of membrane, the incorporation of membrane with hydrophilic materials such as inorganic metal oxides (MOs) has proposed to be one of the most auspicious strategy to increase membrane hydrophilicity. Among the inorganic metal oxides, TiO2 with superior photocatalytic and superhydrophilicity has make it an attractive material to modify the polymer membrane [6]. The construction of membrane with excellent hydration behavior and steric hindrance could effectively inhibit the close contact and adhesion of organic pollutant on the membrane surface [7]. Meanwhile, the immobilized photocatalysts could further oxidize the pollutant molecules deposited on it. TiO2 could impart photocatalytic properties when irradiated with UV light or light with energy equal or greater than its band gap. Although various studies on TiO2/PAA/polymer photocatalytic membrane have been investigated, their photocatalytic function can only be put into full play under UV illumination [8]. Chi et al. [9] and Qian et al. [10] reported that although the TiO2 functional layer can improve the surface hydrophilicity and antifouling properties of PTFE membrane, their self-cleaning or degradation ability can only be realized under UV irradiation owing to the large band gap (3.2 eV). Therefore, coating of visible-light responsive materials onto the membrane surface is highly desired to widen the application of TiO2 in both UV and visible light region. The utilization of visible light that accounts a larger portion of the sunlight could further reduce the energy and maintenance cost. Various materials have been coupled with TiO2 to enhance the visible light absorption such as graphene oxide [11] and carbon nitride [9]. In this study, combining TiO2 with nanosized CQDs extracted from biomass is particularly viable due to its facile preparation steps and the green precursor used.

To realize visible-light responsive photocatalysis, the coupling of TiO2 with carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has become a research hotspot in the past decade [12,13]. CQDs are categorized as zero dimensional carbon nanomaterials with size less than 10 nm. CQDs has received increasing attention in modifying the band structure of TiO2 as compared to other carbon materials such as activated carbon, fullerene, carbon nanotube and graphene. This is because the practical applicability of the above materials was largely limited by the expensive precursors used and the tedious pre-treatment steps involved in the production. By assembling CQDs on TiO2, the new heterostructure with tuned band edges could extend light absorption spectra to visible light, and thus generate more free radicals for degradation reaction [14,15]. Also, CQDs can function simultaneously as electron reservoir which direct the preferential flow of excited electrons across the heterojunction due to their high electron-accepting and storing ability. As a result, the rapid separation of electron-hole pairs inhibits them from recombine and return to the equilibrium state.

Moreover, numerous studies revealed that the functional groups on CQDs may impart excellent hydrophilic properties to the TiO2 semiconductor, therefore the immobilization of CQDs/TiO2 could further enhance the antifouling properties of composite membrane [16,17]. Another benefit of integrated membrane is that it can eliminate the photocatalyst separation process and increase their reutilization rate. Since most of the particles size are generally in nano range, the recovery of photocatalyst from treated water either by membrane filtration [18] or magnetic separation [19] is energy-intensive and cost-effective. Hence, the attachment of visible-light responsive material is deemed as a viable solution to alleviate the drawbacks associated with conventional membrane and meanwhile, the additives could also impart self-cleaning capability to the composite membrane [9,10].

The immobilization of nanoparticles on membrane can be performed by surface coating or blending method [20]. The former appears as a suitable way for photocatalytic membrane as the photocatalysts are deposited on the membrane surface and are easily accessible to the pollutants. Another potential advantage is that the pristine membrane morphology (porosity, roughness, mechanical strength) will not be destructed as compared to catalyst blending [21]. Although the surface self-assembly method is more effective in maintaining photocatalytic and antifouling ability, this method requires surface functional groups which serve as binding sites to stably holding the nanoparticles via covalent interaction [8]. Generally, chemical modification of polymer surface could provide functional groups (-COOH, SO2OH, and ether bond) for the binding of TiO2 via covalent bonding or hydrogen bonding [2]. In the past few years, chemical grafting and polymerization method has been widely adopted owing to its simple technique and better control on the degree of functionalization [7,11,22]. You et al. [8] employed the plasma-grafting and self-assembly method in the fabrication of self-cleaning TiO2 /PAA/PVDF membrane. Tran et al. [11] also reported successful grafting of titania and graphene oxide (GO) mixed powder on the plasma-activated PVDF membrane. Results showed that the nanoparticles were well-immobilized and dispersed on the membrane surface, hence proving the effectiveness of this modification technique. Similar method was applied by Qian et al. [10] to modify the surface of hydrophobic polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) membrane. The result evidenced the tight bounding of TiO2 via chelating bidentate coordination and the surface hydrophilicity was greatly enhanced without degradation in 30-day continuous filtration operations.

Generally, the graft polymerization method can be conducted through 3 sequential steps which involved surface activation, PAA graft-polymerization and self-assembly of photocatalysts. Prior to surface modification, membrane is pretreated using active plasma [5,10] or UV irradiation [23,24] to generate high-energy species for the breaking of polymer bonds without destroying the surface structure. In the following step, the pretreated membrane support is immersed into acrylic acid (AA) solution containing initiator and cross-linking agent. The cleaved bonds and free radical species then trigger and initiate in-situ polymerization of AA monomers to form a thin film of polyacrylic acid (PAA). The improved functionality of membrane then facilitates the self-assembly of TiO2 nanoparticles during the dip-coating of membrane in TiO2 suspension. The Ti cations would coordinate with the -COOH binding sites provided by PAA to form strong covalent bonds, either by ion coordination or hydrogen bonding [2]. This approach is potentially advantageous as the grafting degree and catalyst loading can be controlled by manipulating various parameters, such as the monomer concentration, exposure time and power of plasma [8].

Herein, we propose for the first time a hybrid membrane by the integration of oil palm frond derived NCQDs-TiO2 with PES membrane support. In this work, surface modification of PES membrane support was realized through UV-induced graft polymerization of PAA prior to the coating of NCQDs-TiO2 photocatalysts via self-assembly method. The composite membranes were prepared with varying concentrations of NCQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite to evaluate the effect of TiO2 loading on the membrane filtration and photocatalytic performance. The membrane filtration performance was assessed in terms of pure water flux, rejection ability, fouling mitigation and self-cleaning activity for MB while its photocatalytic capability were examined through degradation of MB under visible-light illumination. The innovative design of new hybrid membrane with multi-modal functionalities not only alleviate fouling, at the same time also allows the parallel removal and degradation of contaminants from wastewater system.

All reagents were of analytical grade and were used without further purification. To prepare NCQDs/TiO2 photocatalyst, isopropanol (IPA), ethylenediamine (EDA) and ethanol were purchased from Merck while Titanium (IV) isopropoxide (TTIP) was supplied by Sigma-Aldrich. The biomass precursor for NCQDs, dated oil palm frond was collected locally from an oil palm estate in Tanjung Tualang, Perak. To prepare membrane support, polyethersulfone (PES) pellet was supplied by Solvay. N-Methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP) which was purchased from Merck was used as solvent while polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) from R&M was used as pore forming agent. The reagents involved in the grafting of membrane such as ethylene glycol (EG), acrylic acid (AA) and potassium persulfate (PP) were purchased from Merck, Sigma-Aldrich and Synerlab, respectively.

The NCQDs/TiO2 nanocomposite was synthesised via simple in-situ hydrothermal method according to the protocols that we have reported earlier [25]. Briefly, TTIP precursor was mixed with isopropanol solution and distilled water to form a white suspension. Then, NCQDs solution was added to the mixture and stirred for 2 h. The resulting mixture was hydrothermally heated in an oven at 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained mixture was centrifuged and filtered to get the composite powders. The resulting NCQDs-TiO2 photocatalyst was then dried in an oven overnight. The NCQDs-TiO2 nanocomposite was hereby designated as NTO. The characteristics of the NTO nanocomposites used in this study have been validated by TEM, SEM, EDX, XRD and FTIR analyse which can be found in our previous study [25].

The PES membrane support was fabricated through phase inversion method. To prepare the membrane casting solution, 17 wt% of PES and 1 wt% of PVP (with respect to PES) were dissolved in 83 wt% of NMP solvent at 60 °C. The casting solution was sealed with parafilm to prevent entering of moisture. The blend solution was stirred at 400 rpm for 6 h to guarantee complete dissolution of the PES pellets and was left overnight for degassing. After degassing, the casting solution was spread uniformly on a clear glass plate with the aid of casting blade adjusted to 200 µm thick. Subsequently, the casted film was immersed immediately into a water coagulation bath at room temperature to allow the exchange of solvent and non-solvent during the phase inversion process. After the film peeled off itself, the fabricated membrane was kept overnight in deionized water (DI) bath to ensure a complete phase inversion and to remove any residual solvent. Finally, the synthesised membrane was stored in a DI water bath before being used for further characterization and surface modification.

In this work, PES membrane was modified using sequential UV-induced graft polymerisation method which involves 3 successive steps: UV-radiated pretreatment of PES, graft polymerization of AA on pretreated PES, and self-assembly of NTO photocatalyst onto the PAA/PES membrane. Prior to surface modification, the PES membrane was rinsed with deionised water to clean the surface. Firstly, the as-prepared PES membrane support was irradiated by a 40 W UV lamp for 15 min to generate free radicals. Thereafter, the activated membrane was immediately immersed in grafting solution for 30 min. Prior to chemical grafting, polymer solution consisting of 20 wt% of AA monomer, 1 wt% of potassium persulfate and ethylene glycol (molar ratio of EG:AA=1:6.5) was prepared. After 30 min of dipping, the residual AA monomer was rolled off and the membrane was sandwiched between two glass plates for drying at 80 °C for 4 h.

In the third step, NTO was self-assembled onto the grafted membrane using a simple dip-coating method. Before dip-coating of membrane, the NTO photocatalyst was first dispersed in 15 ml of distilled water and ultrasonicated for 30 min to prevent aggregation of the nanocomposite. The grafted membranes were then dipped in NTO colloidal suspension with varying concentrations (0.1, 0.3 and 0.5 wt%) for 30 min. The dipping allowed the NTO nanocomposite to self-assemble on the surface-grafted PAA/PES membrane. Finally, the composite membranes were dipped one more time in DI to eliminate weakly bound NTO particles prior drying in the oven at 80 °C for 12 h. The composite membranes were thus formed and denoted as NTO-0.1/PAA/PES, NTO-0.3/PAA/PES and NTO-0.5/PAA/PES for UV-induced grafted membrane coated with 0.1, 0.3, and 0.5 wt% NTO, respectively. The overall process for the fabrication of NTO/PAA/PES composite membrane is illustrated in Fig. 1.

The surface morphology and cross-sectional structure of the synthesised membranes were observed on a scanning electron microscope equipped with energy dispersive X-ray (SEM-EDX) (Hitachi, S-3400N). The membrane specimens were sputter coated with gold before SEM observation. EDX was employed to identify the surface elemental composition of the prepared membranes. The chemical composition of the membranes was investigated by using Fourier transform infrared (FTIR, Nicolet, IS10) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The membrane surface hydrophilicity was analysed by measuring the static water contact angle (SWCA) over the membrane surface using contact angle meter (FM40Mk2, KR
U
̈
SS GmbH).

Photocatalytic study was performed by selecting MB as the model pollutant to evaluate the degradation performances of NTO/PAA/PES modified membranes under visible light illumination. In a batch mode reaction system, membrane with an effective area of 14.6 cm2 were suspended in 50 ml of MB solution (5 mg/L) under stirring condition. The beaker was positioned under visible-light irradiation illuminated from 80 W LED lamp. Prior to light irradiation, the suspended membrane was kept stirring under dark condition for 30 min so that adsorption-desorption equilibrium was established between the pollutants and membrane surface. During photocatalysis, 4 ml of aliquot was withdrawn every 15 min and the dye concentration was measured by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (PG Instruments T60) at a wavelength of 663 nm. The photodegradation of MB using pristine PES and PAA-grafted membranes were also evaluated for comparison purpose. The decolorization efficiency was computed according to Eq. (1):

To investigate the reusability of the composite membrane, cyclic-degradation experiment was conducted using the best degradation performance composite membrane. After the first run of photocatalysis, the used membrane was rinsed with distilled water for several times to remove any adsorbed dye. The MB degradation was then repeated for two more cycles under the visible-light irradiation. The triplicates repeating of the degradation process could also study the stability of the photocatalysts on the PES support.

The flux performance of the prepared membranes was assessed through pure water flux (PWF) experiment conducted in a dead-end filtration cell filled with distilled water. Before the filtration process, the membrane with a total filtration area of 14.6 cm2 was compacted at a transmembrane pressure (TMPs) of 4 bar for 15 min. Thereafter, the filtration process was initiated at 2 bar and the weight of permeate was recorded continuously over time for 15 min. The pure water flux, J
w of the membranes were computed according to Darcy law as expressed in Eq. (3):

Membrane rejection ability was evaluated by filtering 10 mg/L of MB solution under an operating pressure of 2 bar and the permeate was collected in a beaker for 30 min. The dye rejection was determined by measuring the MB absorbance of permeate using the UV–vis spectrophotometer. The rejection of MB solutes was calculated using Eq. (4):

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of antifouling and self-cleaning performance of membranes
ce:section.ce:para:
The antifouling performance of membranes was evaluated by continuous filtering 10 mg/L of MB solution at 2 bar for 1 h. The permeate flux was recorded every 1 min until equilibrium flux was established. This was to observe the flux decline rate of membranes during the fouling process. After the stable flux was achieved, the feed solution was replaced with distilled water and the pure water flux of the fouled membrane (J
w1 ) was measured. The original pure water flux (J
w ) obtained in Section 2.7 was used as a baseline to determine the decrement of filtration capability after fouling. The flux reduction of membrane was computed using Eq. (5):
(5)
Flux reduction =

To investigate the self-cleaning ability of membranes, the fouled membranes were regenerated via two cleaning modes, which includes physical cleaning using distilled water and visible-light irradiation for 15 min. The cleaned membranes were then permeated with distilled water to obtain pure water fluxes of the recovered membranes (J
w2 ). The relative flux recovery ratio (FRR) was computed using Eq. (6):

SEM was conducted to study the surface morphology and structural evolution of bare PES membrane after surface modification. Fig. 2 displays the plane view of the pristine PES, PAA/PES and different loading of NTO/PAA/PES composite membranes at a magnification of ×6 k. As seen, the pristine PES membrane exhibited smooth surface with uniformly distributed pores. The clearly visible pores signified the rapid mass transfer between NMP and water during the precipitation process [26]. After the graft polymerization, no significant change was detected except that the pores became less visible and the membrane surface getting relatively rougher. Besides, an extra film was seem to have formed on the membrane surface which covered the pores developed (see Fig. 2(b)). It was anticipated that the polymerization of AA monomers has been initiated which promoted the growth of chemical cross-linking on the membrane surface, which will be further proved below. The grafted PAA layer introducing key functionalization sites which is necessary for the immobilization of photocatalysts through covalent interaction [2].

Fig. 2(c)–(e) reveal the successful coating of NTO nanoparticles on the membrane surfaces via self-assembly method. This is a physical evidence to confirm the presence of carboxylic sites provided by PAA for the attachment of NTO either via coordinance bonding or hydrogen bonding. At a low NTO loading, the nanocomposites were randomly dispersed on the membrane surface. While at higher loading, the ultrafine nanoparticles with high surface area to particle size ratio tended to form aggregates and nano-islands in certain area. When the NTO loading increased further to 0.5 wt%, the nanoparticles get agglomerated which gave rise to a dense and compact NTO layer over the entire surface. At this stage, the PAA/PES surface has been well-masked by photocatalysts, hence most of the pores could no longer be observed. Moreover, some nanoparticles that adhered within the pore wall would eventually cause rampant pore blockage. The developed chains together with the deposited photocatalyst layer would improve membrane selectivity, however, the pore plugging would also cause passive result to membrane permeability.

The elemental composition of the prepared membranes was detected using EDX analysis. As shown in Table 1, the pristine PES membrane was mainly composed of carbon, oxygen, sulfur and a small amount of nitrogen elements which was consistent with the previous study [27]. The PAA-modified membrane also possessed the similar major elemental composition except that the atomic percentage of oxygen elements was slightly higher, which corresponded to the presence of AA [28]. As for the composite membranes, the presence of NTO nanocomposites was evidenced by the newly recorded Ti signal, which are in compliance with FTIR and XPS spectroscopic data. Accordingly, the atomic fraction of O and N elements also increased slightly. The Ti, O and N content increased proportionally with the NTO loading. The Ti element that introduced onto the membrane surfaces signified the binding of nanocomposites was well-established using self-assembly method. Fig. 3 depicts the distribution of each element on the membrane structure. The EDX mapping demonstrates a homogeneous dispersion of Ti element across the membrane surface, indicating the even coating of nanocomposites on the membrane via self-assembly method.

An inspection of the surface chemical composition of the pristine PES membrane and composite membranes were performed using FTIR and XPS analyses. As shown in Fig. 4, the FTIR spectrum of pristine membrane presents the standard peaks of PES at 1105 cm−1, 1150 cm−1 and 1322 cm−1, 1241 cm−1, 1486 cm−1 and 1577 cm−1 which associated to C‒O bond stretching [29,30], symmetric and asymmetric stretching of sulfone group [31,32], aromatic phenylene ether stretching [30,31], C‒C bond stretching [29,30] and C˭C aromatic skeletal vibration, respectively. To verify the graft polymerisations of AA, the surface bonding of PAA/PES membrane has been intensively studied. As shown in Fig. 4, the peaks corresponding to the PES support layer was observed. In addition, a new peak was observed at 1714 cm−1 which associated to the carboxylic groups (C˭O) from PAA/PES membrane [9,33]. The emerged peak authenticate the effective grafting of PAA barrier layer onto the PES surface via simple surface covalent bonding [34]. A similar observation has been reported in previous studies [35,36].

After the deposition of NTO photocatalyst, the most distinctive change caused by NTO binding was the new vibration band centred at 3400 cm−1 which attributed to the –OH stretching of TiO2. As the NTO loading increased, the band intensity was strengthened due to the increasing content of hydroxyl groups that showed high affinity to surrounding water molecules [9]. This would endow the membrane with excellent hydrophilic property. Moreover, the enhancement of peak might be ascribed to the hydrogen bonding (O–H…O) formed in metal hydroxides (Ti–OH) after being deposited on the grafted membrane. As pointed out in Isawi et al. [37] work, the distinctive stretching vibration of Ti–O–Ti and Ti–O are supposed to be appeared at 630 cm−1 and 1460 cm−1, respectively. However, the representative peaks of TiO2 were indistinguishable in this study which probably due to the low concentration of NTO with respect to the PAA grafted surface. Instead, an obvious recession of peaks below 1000 cm−1 was observed, which was analogous to those reported in previous studies [8,28]. The broad absorption at low frequency (400–1000 cm−1) was most likely attributed to the overlapping peaks of Ti–O–Ti and Ti–O vibration modes.

With the increase in NTO loading, the C˭O vibration of PAA at 1714 cm−1 was found diminished yet a new peak was observed at 1637 cm−1. The appearance of this peak was probably due to the overlapping of C˭O characteristic peaks of NCQDs [14] and the –OH bending vibration of attached TiO2. The loss of C˭O bond character together with the augmented vibration of –OH bending validated the NTO has been immobilized on the PAA membrane surface. In fact, the interaction between PAA polymers and NTO was realized through the formation of hydrogen bonding (O–H…O) between the C˭O groups and the hydroxyl groups on TiO2 surface [8,37]. The results coincided with the binding scheme reported by Shi et al. [2] who outlined that TiO2 could either form hydrogen bond with COOH groups or could bound with oxygen atoms via coordination to the Ti4+ cation. It is well accepted that the composite membranes prepared via simple self-assembly method possess excellent stability since the functional groups of NTO were still detected after being immersed in DI water for two weeks prior to the FTIR analysis. The stable bonding of NTO is an essential requirement for photocatalytic membrane so it will not be dislodged by intensive shear forces during membrane filtration and photocatalysis.

To further investigate the variation of chemical species on the composite membrane, the chemical composition of NTO-0.5/PAA/PES composite membrane was characterized using XPS. As shown in Fig. 5, the wide scan spectrum shows the characteristic peaks of C, O, S and N elements, which were in good agreement with the theoretical composition of PES membrane [38,39]. Meanwhile, the O1s and Ti2p signal were clearly observed and these peaks dominate the survey spectra of NTO composite membrane. The upmost O1s peak was mainly attributed to the surface oxygen moieties originated from the PAA sub-layer and the NTO coating [9,10]. Meanwhile, the strong Ti2p peak revealed the firm deposition of NTO nanocomposite on PES membrane. This also explained the relatively weak C1s peak as the PES surface has limited exposure after being masked by PAA and NTO active layer. The UV-induced graft polymerization followed by self-assembly is a versatile approach to improve surface functionalization of polymer support for NTO coating.

The high resolution spectra (C1s, O1s, Ti2p curve fittings) of the modified membrane were further analysed to study the chemical states of the atoms. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the C1s core level spectrum was curved-fitted into 5 main absorption peaks. The two strong peaks centred at about 284.50 eV and 285.46 eV were well-assigned to C–C and C–S binding of PES membrane, respectively. The minor peak at a binding energy of 286.45 eV was associated to the ether groups (C–O–C) of PES membrane [29,40]. Meanwhile, the two extra peaks observed at 287.70 eV and 288.73 eV were corresponded to C˭O and COOH functional groups of PAA, respectively [29,41]. These results were in compliance with the FTIR data and reaffirmed the existence of PAA cross-linking on the PES membrane.

Moreover, the narrow scan of O1s and T2p were also recorded. In the O1s core level spectrum depicted in Fig. 6(b), the major peak centring at 529.55 eV was ascribed to the lattice oxygen atom in TiO2 while the peaks at 531.11 eV and 532.57 eV represented the oxygen species in sulfone (SO2) and ether groups of PES membrane, respectively [10]. TiO2 as the major contributor of oxygen atoms once again proved the binding of inorganic modifiers on the PES support. In the Ti2p spectrum, two major components, Ti 2p1/2 (464.02 eV) and Ti 2p3/2 (458.32 eV) with a binding energy split of 5.7 eV were noted, which was in accordance with the results reported in previous literature [9].

Surface hydrophilicity is an important index to determine the water permeability and antifouling properties of membrane. Fig. 7 presents the water contact angle of the membranes. As shown, the control PES membrane showed the highest water contact angle of 69.50º, testifying their intrinsic hydrophobic character which is more susceptible to membrane fouling. After the surface grafting of PAA, the contact angle of PAA/PES dropped down to 49.86º. This signified that the grafting of PAA has endowed the pristine PES membrane with promoted hydrophilicity. The enhanced wettability of PAA-functionalized surface was attributed to the existence of hydrophilic polar groups (carboxyl and hydroxyl) as evidenced in FTIR and XPS spectra. After coated with different amount of NTO nanocomposite, the contact angle of membrane reduced proportionally with the photocatalyst loading and reached a lowest value of 27.57º. The enhanced contact angle was due to the large number of hydrophilic entities (–OH groups) contributed by the inorganic nanocomposite [4]. Although NTO additives could promote the surface hydrophilicity of PES, the contact angle only improved slightly at higher dosage of NTO (0.3 and 0.5 wt%). This suggested that the NTO nanoparticles might have agglomerated which reduced the surface area of photocatalyst available on the membrane surface, hence resulted in minor enhancement in hydrophilicity [42].

The considerable decrement of contact angle has proven the effectiveness of self-assembling technique in enhancing the surface hydrophilicity of PES. With reference to the study by Li et al. [43], the hydrophilic improvement brought by self-assembly method is more pronounced than that of blending method. The author reported a slight reduction of contact angle (from ~67º to 52º) when TiO2 particles were blended in the casting solution. The entrapped TiO2 particles within the membrane matrix have limited their exposure to the surrounding water molecules, hence resulting in low hydrophilicity. On the contrary, an interface layer of water molecules could be formed on the surface-coated TiO2 which acts as a barrier to inhibit hydrophobic molecules. The superior membrane hydrophilicity could ensure excellent antifouling performance and promise their application in membrane filtration, which was further verified in the succeeding sections.

Generally, the enhancement of surface hydrophilicity would lead to higher water flux owing to the decrease resistance of water permeation through the pores. However, the degree of hydrophilicity is disproportionate to the water flux, as depicted in Fig. 8. All the modified membranes experienced a considerable decline in water flux which did not follow the contact angle trend. This shows that surface hydrophilicity is not the dominant factor determining the flux behaviour of membrane. After the immobilization of photocatalysts, the flux values of composite membranes continue to drop with sequential increase in nanocomposite loading. The results obtained were almost similar to the previous study [29]. You et al. [8] pointed out that surface grafting may alter the membrane morphology and reduce the porosity of membrane, thereby leading to inevitable flux decline of surface modified membrane. Similarly in this case, the polymeric network coated on and inside the membrane pores may increase the thickness and reduce the pore size of membrane. This eventually increased the hydraulic resistance of the modified membrane [44,45]. The significant reduction of flux performance was probably due to the clogging of pores by the NTO aggregates which conformed to the SEM images. The rampant blockage of pores would reduce the effective vacant pores for the free flow of water [46]. Despite the significant reduction of water flux, the lowest flux value attained by NTO-0.5/PAA/PES, which was 22.5 L/m2 h (also equivalent to 11.25 L/m2 h bar) still within the permeance range of ultrafiltration membrane. This was defined with reference to the study by Ren et al. [47] who claimed that ultrafiltration membranes have a good permeance of approximately ten to hundreds of L/m2·h·bar. Although the surface modification has a negative impact on membrane permeability, the surface grafting layer is extremely conducive to other pertinent properties such as rejection of foulants and antifouling ability of membrane.

As shown in Fig. 8, the pristine PES showed the lowest MB rejection (46.6%) whereas the NTO-0.5/PAA/PES membrane attained the highest rejection capability (90.9%). Membrane performance is usually constrained by the trade-off between permeability and selectivity [46]. As seen, the opposing trend between the two key parameters revealed that the modified membranes favour rejection, while opposing permeability. With the deposition of supporting structure and active layer, the mesh-like intra cross linking of PAA and the deposited nanocomposite have covered the surface and fill the inner pores of membranes, hence resulted in smaller pore size and enhanced rejection performance [48]. In addition, the better rejection performance is also derived from the enhanced surface hydrophilicity of NTO/PAA/PES membranes. The hindrance layer formed which attributed to the NTO functional layer would weaken the adsorption of hydrophobic dye molecules and hinder the transport of pollutants across the membrane [47]. This was supported by the water contact angle measurements in Fig. 7. Therefore, the modified membranes can achieve high rejection efficiency and at the same time maintained acceptable flux. All in all, the results weighted the merits and faults of using chemical graft-polymerization technique and is suggested to be an effective modification solution that can guarantee both rejection and permeance of membrane.

The antifouling performances of the prepared membranes were evaluated in the continuous filtration of MB solution under dark condition. Fig. 9 shows the relative flux profiles (J/Jo) of membranes wherein the flux ratio at certain time was derived with respect to the initial flux. As observed, the fouling behaviour of all membranes were almost identical, with fast initial deterioration and low steady flux. However, the filtration time required to achieve flux stabilization was different. It was demonstrated that the PAA-grafted membrane achieved steady flux at a shorter filtration time while for the composite membranes, the duration needed further decreased. The filtration period decreased in the order of PES (40 min) > PAA/PES (25 min) > NTO-0.1/PAA/PES (20 min) > NTO-0.3/PAA/PES (15 min) > NTO-0.5/PAA/PES (10 min). The sudden decline in flux, which is a typical fouling behaviour was due to immediate blockage of pores caused by the adsorption of MB foulants. The gradually thickened and compacted cake layer eventually caused the flux to reduce steadily and approached to a stable state [7].

The fastest flux stabilization attained by NTO-0.5/PAA/PES membrane indicated the enhanced antifouling capacity at higher dosage of photocatalysts. The retarded fouling rate in PAA-grafted membrane was due to the polymeric barrier that blocked the passage of foulants through the chain network [8]. In addition, the hydrophilic carboxylic groups of PAA would interact with water molecules via H-bonding and Van der Waals force. This hindered the sedimentation or attachment of hydrophobic MB molecules on the surface and prevent the inner pore-plugging of membrane [3,22]. Similarly, the superhydrophilic nature of NTO would further strengthened the membrane resistance to foulants due to the high surface tension of boundary that make it difficult for the organic foulants to approach and deposit on the membrane [4,5].

After the fouling test, the MB-fouled membranes were subjected to cleaning process for membrane recovery, including physical washing and visible-light illumination. As depicted in Fig. 10, the flux recovery ratio (FRR) obtained from the two cleaning modes were compared so as to assess the self-regeneration capability of the membranes. For the manual cleaning mode, a low FFR ratio (33.8%) was observed in the pristine membrane. The slight recovery was due to the tight bounding of foulants on the membrane and inside the pores that was hardly detached from the membrane [4]. For the PAA/PES membrane, the FRR increased moderately to 46.9%, which might be due to the polymeric network that formed a loosely bounded fouling layer. The FRR value continued to increase for composite membranes and the NTO-0.5/PAA/PES membrane recorded the highest recovery ratio of 83.9%. As a high percentage of foulants could be detached by shear force, it can be thus assumed that the foulants were loosely bounded and the composite membranes suffered from reversible fouling [7]. Despite the high FRR values, the flux recovery via physical washing was not effective enough to retrieve the original flux of the membrane.

As can be seen in Fig. 10, the water fluxes of fouled NTO-modified membranes were intensively revived after being exposed to visible light for 15 min. The FRR increased considerably with NTO loading and reached the highest value of 93.7% for NTO-0.5/PAA/PES membrane, which almost recovered its initial flux. The visible light illumination has activated the photocatalyst and trigger the self-cleaning ability of photocatalytic membrane. By applying irradiation, the free radicals generated by photo-excited electron-hole pairs are responsible to break the foulants bond that stubbornly adhered on the surface and inside the pores. The detached MB molecules was subsequently decomposed into non-hazardous substances [5,22]. Also, the photo-induced excitons could interact with surface-adsorbed water molecules to form surface hydroxyl groups (-OH). These superhydrophilic groups would weaken the binding and repel the contaminants settled on the surface, thus they can be washed off easily during the rinsing process [10]. It can be noticed that the FFR values derived from the two cleaning modes were almost similar for both pristine PES and PAA/PES, revealing that neither PES nor PAA/PES exhibited the self-cleaning property [9]. Therefore, it is reasonable to conclude that the visible-responsive NTO offered the membrane with self-cleaning capability through visible light reaction.

The photocatalytic capability of membranes was evaluated through the degradation of MB dye under visible-light irradiation, as depicted in Fig. 11. As shown, the decolorisation of MB by photolysis alone is negligible. Prior to the visible-light illumination, the membranes experienced different degree of MB reduction which can be related to the MB adsorption on the membrane surfaces. It was observed that the time required for PES and PAA/PES membranes to establish adsorption equilibrium was shorter (less than 30 min) with respect to the NTO-deposited membranes. The higher adsorption capacity of NTO composite membranes was due to the presence of opposite charges between the intrinsic negative charge of TiO2 and the cationic MB molecules [4]. Meanwhile, the deposition of NTO nanocomposites has increased the specific surface area of membranes, thus more adsorption sites were available for MB adsorption [5]. Therefore, it can be concluded that the removal of dye under dark condition was mainly contributed by interfacial adsorption under the influence of electrostatic attraction.

Upon visible-light illumination, the MB removal observed over the pristine and PAA membrane were almost similar to that under dark condition. Both PES and PAA/PES membranes did not exhibit photocatalytic capability due to the absence of photocatalysts. On the other hand, the composite membranes with 0.1 wt%, 0.3 wt% and 0.5 wt% NTO loading showed superb degradation performance, wherein 72.9%, 84.9% and 94.9% of MB were decomposed, respectively. The rapid removal rate of composite membranes can be assigned to the synergy effect of membrane adsorption and photocatalysis. However, the higher degree of reduction under light irradiation unravelled that the dye removal can be predominantly attributed to NTO photocatalysis [8]. It is worth stressing that the best dye removal efficiency was observed in the membrane with highest NTO coverage. The increased active sites for dye adsorption and the close proximity of pollutants with catalyst surface eventually speed up the overall degradation rate [49]. Besides, more free radicals would be generated to attack the MB organic compounds when NTO loading was increased [5]. In this section, the responsiveness of NTO to visible light has been proven to activate the self-cleaning capability of composite membrane.

In order to have a deep insight on the origination of photocatalytic ability, a plausible mechanism for the visible-light driven photocatalysis of NTO nanocomposites was explained as follows. Upon visible light exposure, the NTO nanocomposites with broad absorption edge would utilize short wavelength light to generate photo-excited electron-hole pairs. The electrons on the conduction band would immediately migrate to the neighbouring NCQDs surface, thereby preventing the recombination of electron-hole pairs [14]. The separated charges will then react with adsorbed oxygen and water molecules to generate reactive oxygen species such as superoxide and hydroxyl radicals, which are highly reactive in decomposing the adsorbed MB pollutants into non-harmful substances [50].

To further assess the photocatalytic efficiency of the membranes, kinetic study was conducted by applying Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) model. The kinetics plots of dye degradation reaction and the apparent rate constants, kapp of all membranes were studied and presented in Fig. 12 and Table 2. As seen, the experimental data was well-fitted in the linear plot, showing the reaction followed the pseudo-first-order kinetic law. The highest reaction rate constant calculated was 0.0397 min−1 for NTO-0.5/PAA/PES composite membrane, which was 36 times of that of pristine PES membrane (0.0011 min−1). A comparative table compiling the apparent rate constants (kapp) of various membranes obtained in previous literature is presented in Table 3. The rate constant in present study is high relative to those reported, considering the comparatively low dosage of photocatalyst in this study. Despite the low pollutant concentration, the composite membrane with excellent photocatalytic performance under visible-light was proven to be more efficient than the reported hybrid membranes.

Moreover, the reusability of the composite membranes were studied by repeating the degradation test for 3 cycles using the optimal NTO-0.5/PAA/PES membrane. The membrane was washed and rinsed with distilled water after each run. As shown in Fig. 13, the MB degradation activity only decrease by a little after the cyclic-degradation experiment, verifying the outstanding stability of the composite membrane. The slight decline of removal efficiency may be attributed to the adhesion of small organic species that was not removed completely in the previous run, thereby retarding the degradation activity in the following cycles [7,11]. Furthermore, the above analysis indirectly proved the strong attachment of photocatalyst on the membrane surface. The steady degradation behaviour is a great promise for the practical application of composite membrane in water purification.

In summary, the NTO/PAA/PES composite membranes were successfully fabricated via UV-induced graft polymerization and self-assembly method. The grafted binding sites and the firm coating of NTO composite on the membrane were evidenced by the presence of –COOH functional groups and Ti elements in FTIR and XPS analysis. The grafting of -COOH groups and self-assembly of NTO dramatically enhanced the surface hydrophilicity, especially when the catalyst loading increased. However, the improved hydrophilicity did not reflect to better water permeability due to the blockage of pores by PAA crosslinking and nanoparticles. Nevertheless, the superhydrophilicity led to loosely bound foulants and thereby giving rise to the improved rejection and antifouling behavior. Moreover, the composite membrane was found to possess superior photocatalysis-assisted self-cleaning property which restored 93.7% of the original flux after illuminated with visible-light. The composite membrane also demonstrated remarkable degradation efficiency, wherein 94.9% of MB was removed after 1 h of visible-light irradiation. The optimal composite membrane, NTO-0.5/PAA/PES which undergone 3 degradation cycles without significant deterioration has proven the stability of bonded composite as well as the consistency of degradation performance. In short, the NTO nanocomposite has endowed the membrane with both photocatalytic and hydrophilic effect, which efficiently mitigate fouling problem and granted the membranes with new high-value functions.
